is_p_falciparum.evidence: Paper explicitly studies Plasmodium falciparum infection. Title and throughout the manuscript refer to 'P. falciparum infection' and malaria parasites in field samples.

reports_target_markers.evidence: The paper does not report any of the curated drug-resistance codon positions. It examines malaria parasitemia prevalence in relation to hemoglobin genotypes (HbSS, HbAS, HbAA) using only blood film microscopy for parasite detection. No molecular genotyping of drug-resistance markers (Pfcrt, Pfmdr1, Pfdhfr, Pfdhps, Pfk13, Pfcytb) is performed or reported.

reports_extractable_frequencies.evidence: No drug-resistance marker frequencies are reported. The study reports only malaria infection prevalence (presence/absence of parasites on blood films) stratified by host hemoglobin genotype: 44.1% in HbAA, 36.2% in HbAS, and 20% in HbSS. These are infection prevalences, not resistance marker frequencies.

sub_country_location.evidence: Study conducted in Kisumu area of Western Kenya near Lake Victoria. Participants are explicitly stated as residents of the Kisumu area, a sub-national location in Kenya. Materials and methods state: 'The study population was West Kenyan residents of the Kisumu area.'

temporal_resolution_within_3y.evidence: Blood samples collected over a 12-month period (stated in Results: 'Blood samples were taken from 773 subjects over a period of 12 months'). This falls within the 3-year maximum window.

african_field_samples.evidence: Samples are from indigenous human field/clinical populations in Kenya (Western Kenya/Kisumu area). Materials and methods specify: 'indigenous patients attending the blood testing laboratory who had resided in the area for at least 6 months or had migrated from an equally endemic area.' These are African field samples, not lab strains or imported cases.

is_primary_study.evidence: This is a primary empirical cross-sectional study with original data collection. The authors conducted a cross-sectional survey, performed blood tests and malaria microscopy on 773 subjects, and reported original results. It is not a review, meta-analysis, or commentary.

duplicate_risk.evidence: This is a single-site, hospital-based cross-sectional study from the Kisumu area conducted over 12 months. No mention is made of samples being part of a multi-site consortium, named cohort, surveillance network, or prior dataset. Standard field collection with routine low overlap risk.

supplement.evidence: The paper does not report drug-resistance marker frequencies, so supplementary molecular data would not change eligibility. The criterion failure is fundamental: no target markers are genotyped or reported anywhere in the document or supplementary materials.

assessment.summary: This cross-sectional study from Kisumu, Kenya examined the relationship between hemoglobin genotype and malaria infection prevalence in 728 field samples collected over 12 months (1997). While it is a primary African field study with appropriate temporal and spatial resolution, it FAILS the core eligibility criterion of reporting target drug-resistance marker frequencies. The study uses only blood film microscopy to detect malaria parasites and stratifies results by host hemoglobin genotype (HbS-related); it does not perform or report molecular genotyping of any of the curated drug-resistance codon positions (Pfcrt, Pfmdr1, Pfdhfr, Pfdhps, Pfk13, Pfcytb). The data are epidemiological (infection prevalence by host genotype), not pharmacogenetic (parasite resistance allele frequencies).
exclusion_reasons: Does not report any of the target drug-resistance marker frequencies (Pfcrt, Pfmdr1, Pfdhfr, Pfdhps, Pfk13, Pfcytb codon positions). Study examines only malaria parasitemia prevalence by host hemoglobin genotype using blood film microscopy; no molecular resistance markers are genotyped.
No extractable per-locus resistance marker frequency data (numerator/denominator or percentages) reported.
